coredata.dc:title: A one-step sonoelectrochemical preparation method of pure blue fluorescent carbon nanoparticles under a high intensity electric field
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2013 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Yao, Su
Hu, Yufei
Li, Gongke
coredata.dc:description: A simple and efficient sonoelectrochemical method was proposed for the preparation of blue fluorescent carbon nanoparticles (CNPs) in pure water. Benefiting from the combination of the high intensity electric field and the ultrasonic field, pure blue fluorescent CNPs were rapidly generated without any complex purification. The obtained CNPs possess excellent fluorescent property and thermodynamic stability in aqueous solution. The effects of the high intensity electric field and the ultrasonic field on the fluorescent property of the electrolytic products were also investigated. Compare to the normal electrolysis, the high intensity electric field played a crucial role in improving the product purity. On the other hand, the electrolytic efficiency was obviously enhanced, since the ultrasonic field could act synergistically with the high intensity electric field. The obtained CNPs have the potential for application in bioimaging, immuno-labeling and fluorescence detection.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Effects of the high intensity electric field and the ultrasonic field

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Scheme of the electrolytic device. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
(A) XPS spectra of CNPs in C 1s region, (B) FT-IR image of CNPs. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectrum of the CNPs’ solution. The emission spectrum was obtained under maximum excitation wavelength of 330nm, and the excitation spectrum was obtained at the maximum emission wavelength of 450nm. Inset: digital photo for the CNPs’ solution, illuminated with white light (left) and UV lamp (right). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

The stability of the CNPs’ solution. (A) Dependence of fluorescence intensity on centrifuge time, the centrifuge speed was 10,000rpm. (B) Dependence of fluorescence intensity on the concentration of NaCl. (C) Fluorescence spectra of the CNPs’ solution (a) and the re-dissolving CNPs’ solution (b). (D) Tyndall effect in the re-dissolving CNPs’ solution. The entire excitation wavelength was 330nm. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

The comparison of the electrolytic products which were obtained under the low or high intensity electric field. (A) Fluorescence spectrum of the electrolytic products in 0.1molL−1 PBS (pH=7.4). The blue line (a) and red line (b) were in the absence and presence of ultrasonic field. The electrolytic voltage was 4V and the ultrasonic power was 200W. The electrolytic time was 20min. The excitation wavelength was 330nm. (B) Fluorescence spectrum of the electrolytic products in pure water. The blue line (a) and red line (b) were in the absence and presence of ultrasonic field. The electrolytic voltage was 100V and the ultrasonic power was 200W. The electrolytic time was 20min. The excitation wavelength was 330nm. Inset: digital photo for the products which were illuminated with UV lamp. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

The synergy effect of the ultrasonic field and the high intensity electric field. (a) Emission spectrum of the CNPs’ solution, (b) Emission spectrum of the CNPs’ solution which were maintained under 100V voltage for 5min without ultrasonic field, (c) Emission spectrum of pure water, in which the anode of (b) was kept in ultrasonic field for 20min, (d) Emission spectrum of pure water, in which the untreated anode was kept under ultrasonic field for 20min. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

head.ce:title: A one-step sonoelectrochemical preparation method of pure blue fluorescent carbon nanoparticles under a high intensity electric field

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Sun Yat-sen University, Guangzhou, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A simple and efficient sonoelectrochemical method was proposed for the preparation of blue fluorescent carbon nanoparticles (CNPs) in pure water. Benefiting from the combination of the high intensity electric field and the ultrasonic field, pure blue fluorescent CNPs were rapidly generated without any complex purification. The obtained CNPs possess excellent fluorescent property and thermodynamic stability in aqueous solution. The effects of the high intensity electric field and the ultrasonic field on the fluorescent property of the electrolytic products were also investigated. Compare to the normal electrolysis, the high intensity electric field played a crucial role in improving the product purity. On the other hand, the electrolytic efficiency was obviously enhanced, since the ultrasonic field could act synergistically with the high intensity electric field. The obtained CNPs have the potential for application in bioimaging, immuno-labeling and fluorescence detection.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent carbon-based nanomaterials such as carbon nanotube [1], nanodiamond [2], fullerene [3] and carbon nanoparticle (CNP) [4] have been widely investigated owing to their large surface area, good hydrophilicity and low cytotoxicity. Particularly, fluorescent CNPs have been applied in immuno-labeling and bioimaging technology [5–8]. Some simple methods such as plasma process [9], submerged arc [10] and vapor deposition [11] have been reported to get large amounts of CNPs. But the obtained CNPs were generally non-fluorescent. In order to obtain fluorescent CNPs, it was necessary to introduce oxygen-containing groups on the surface of the CNPs.

Chemical oxidation was commonly used for the preparation of fluorescent CNPs, which almost originated from the carbon-based materials [12–17]. The carbon source such as carbon soot, carbon nanotube and non-fluorescent CNPs have been refluxed in nitric acid for 12h at least, before dialysis and centrifugation were added to purify the obtained fluorescent CNPs. The fluorescent CNPs could be also prepared by electrochemical oxidation [18–20]. In the electrochemical method, graphite anode was electrooxidized in electrolyte solution such as phosphate buffer solution (PBS) for several hours. The other preparation methods including controlled carbonization [21–23], thermal pyrolysis [24], hydrothermal oxidation [25,26], microwave pyrolysis [27,28] have been reported for the generation of fluorescent CNPs. In these methods, the products were almost generated from carbonification of some carbon-containing compounds such as sucrose, citric acid and glycine, which were regarded as the precursor of CNPs. It was worth mentioning that the ultrasonic method has been applied for the preparation of CNPs, since it can ensure the carbonization [29] or surface oxidation [30] in room temperature. However, these methods required at least several hours for the preparation, and the introduced reagents lead to further purification process.

In order to get pure fluorescent CNPs easily, the avoidance of salt in the oxidation should be taken into consideration. Some complex and expensive purification such as electrophoresis, ultrafilter and dialysis have been used to remove salts after the oxidation. Fortunately, it was unessential to introduce salt during electrochemical oxidation. In fact, the electrochemical oxidation could occur in a low concentration of electrolyte solution, even in pure water. However, compared with the normal electrolysis in the high concentration electrolyte solution, the current of the electrolysis was weak in pure water for its huge resistance. Therefore, for an appropriate current in the normal electrolytic device, it was necessary to provide a high electrolytic voltage. In Cui’s research [31], at the beginning of the 7days’ electrolysis, a 5mA current was generated under a 900V voltage. Their products contained CNPs, graphene and mellitic acid. But the fluorescent property of their products was not mentioned. Actually, it was difficult to achieve a very high voltage. However, there was another feasible method in order to achieve the appropriate electrolytic current in the pure water. The electrolytic current was depended on the electric field intensity. So, the distance between the anode and cathode should be significantly decreased, if the electrolytic voltage was limited at an accessible level.

In our paper, a novel electrolytic device was designed and manufactured for the preparation of fluorescent CNPs. It was peculiar with a very narrow gap of 2mm between the anode and cathode. The electric field intensity offered was even higher than that in a normal capillary electrophoresis. Meanwhile, ultrasonic effect was also recommended into the electrolytic process. Based on the synergy effect of the high intensity electric field and the ultrasonic field, the pure blue fluorescent CNPs were generated without any salts. It takes only 20min by the proposed electrolytic method to produce the CNPs. The obtained CNPs possessed excellent fluorescent property and thermodynamic stability in aqueous solution. The synergy effect of the high intensity electric field and the ultrasonic field was further discussed in the paper.

The electrolysis was occurred in a homemade electrolytic device. The scheme of the electrolytic device was shown in Fig. 1 . The cylindrical electrolyzer body with 15mL volume was made from polymethyl methacrylate (PMMA). A spectrum pure graphite ring anode and a titanium tube cathode were placed in the center of electrolyzer. The anode and cathode were separated by an insulative O-ring. The ultrasonic generator was placed through the titanium tube cathode. The pure water was used as electrolyte. The graphite anode’s external diameter was 20mm with its inner diameter and height of 12 and 15mm respectively. Its working area was about 77mm2.

In order to enable the electrolysis under the high intensity electric field and ultrasonic field, the shapes of the anode, cathode and O-ring were carefully designed. The hollow shape of cathode ensured that the ultrasonic power acted on the graphite anode. The thin O-ring with a thickness of 2mm led to a high intensity electric field. And the hollow shape of the anode made the high intensity electric field almost uniform.

All the other chemicals were of analytical grade. The electrolytic voltage was supplied from a ZHAOXIN KXN-3002D DC power source. A XINZHI JY92-II ultrasonic disruptor was used to provide the ultrasonic field. A TECNAI G2 transmission electron microscope (TEM) was used to investigate the surface morphology of the electrolytic product. A NICOLET AVATAR 330 Fourier transform infrared spectrometer and an ESCALAB 250 X-ray photoelectron spectrometer were used to investigate the surface groups of the electrolytic products. The X-ray photoelectron spectrometer was equipped with the Mono AlKα X-ray radiation as the source for excitation at a pressure of less than 2×10−9 mbar in the chamber. The fluorescence spectra were measured by a PERKIN-ELMER LS-45 spectrofluorometer. The UV–vis spectra were measured by a PGENERAL T-6 spectrophotometer. The optical images were shot by a CANON A-720IS digital camera.

The applied electrolytic voltage and the ultrasonic power ensured the ultrasonic electrolysis. The electrolytic voltage was fixed at 100V, and the ultrasonic power was 200W. After the ultrasonic electrolysis for 20min, a light yellow solution was obtained. In order to avoid the influence of the small amount of insoluble substance such as graphite fragments. The product was dried and re-solved for the measure of quantum yield.

In order to get fluorescent CNPs effectively, the results including electrolytic voltage, ultrasonic power and electrolytic time were shown in Fig. S1 of the supporting information. Seen from the Fig. S1A, the fluorescent intensity of the electrolytic products was increased with the increase of electrolytic voltage. But the high electrolytic voltage leads to high Joule heat. When the electrolytic voltage was higher than 120V, the water in the device was easily boiling in the electrolysis. So the electrolytic voltage was limited to 100V. As shown in Fig. S1B, the fluorescent intensity of the electrolytic products reached the maximum value at the ultrasonic power of 200W. When the ultrasonic power was higher than 200W, some black graphite powder was exfoliated in the product solution. So the ultrasonic power of 200W was chosen. As shown in Fig. S1C, the fluorescent intensity of the electrolytic products was increased with the increase of electrolytic time. But when the electrolytic time was longer than 20min, the electrolytic current rose rapidly and some non-fluorescent brown products were generated in the product solution. So the electrolytic last for 20min. The average current was about 20mA.

TEM, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) have been used to investigate the morphology and the surface groups of the CNPs. As shown in Fig. 2 , the TEM image demonstrated that the product was mainly in the form of CNPs. The scale of the CNPs was about 2–3nm. The result of XPS (Fig. S2 in the supporting information) showed that the CNPs contained carbon and oxygen element. The atom ratios of carbon and oxygen were about 62.0% and 38.0%, respectively. In Fig. 3 A, the C1s spectrum of XPS consisted of three peaks at 284.9, 286.3 and 289.1eV, corresponding to the C–C, C–O and –COOH bond. The mass fraction of carboxyls in CNPs was about 52%. According to the FT-IR spectrum of CNPs in Fig. 3B, the most characteristic features were the absorption bands corresponding to the CO of carboxyl stretching at 1625cm−1, the O–H deformation vibration at 1434cm−1 and the C–O stretching of epoxide group at 1064cm−1. The O–H stretching vibrations of the –OH group of CNPs and water appeared at 3403cm−1 as broad adsorption bands [32]. The result of XPS and FT-IR exhibited that abundant carboxyls existed on the surface of CNPs.

As shown in Fig. 4 , the CNPs’ solution emitted bright blue fluorescence, when it was irradiated by a UV lamp. The maximum fluorescence emission spectra of CNPs showed a maximum fluorescent intensity at 450nm, while the maximum excitation wavelength was at 330nm. The emission peak of CNPs would not shift (Fig. S3 in the supporting information), when excitation wavelength was varied. The quantum yield of the re-solving CNPs was observed to be 8.9% (Fig. S4 in the supporting information). The CNPs also showed excellent photostability, since its fluorescence intensity did not change after continuous excitation for 300min (Fig. S5 in the supporting information).

It was remarkable that the obtained CNPs’ solution were thermodynamically stable. Its fluorescence intensity would not reduce after 60min of continuous centrifugation at 10,000rpm (Fig. 5 A). The fluorescence intensity also kept constant when 3molL−1 NaCl was added to the solution (Fig. 5B). When the CNPs solution was dried, it could be re-dissolved in water without stirring or ultrasonic dispersing. The fluorescence intensity of the re-dissolving CNPs’ solution still remained unchanged (Fig. 5C). In addition, the Tyndall effect was also observed in the re-dissolving CNPs’ solution (Fig. 5D). Therefore, the obtained CNPs’ solution was a real solution rather than dispersion. The excellent stability of CNPs’ aqueous solution should be attributed to its abundant carboxyl groups.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of the high intensity electric field and the ultrasonic field
ce:section.ce:para: The pure CNPs was obtained in the electrolysis by the combination of the high intensity electric field and the ultrasonic field. The effects of the high intensity electric field and the ultrasonic field on the fluorescent property of the electrolytic products were further investigated. The electrolytic products were produced under different electrolytic conditions involving in the electric field and the ultrasonic field, and their fluorescent property was studied in detail.

For the comparison of the product obtained under the different electric intensity, PBS was selected as an electrolyte in a low intensity electric field to get similar current density of a high intensity electric field. As shown in Fig. 6 A, when the electrolysis occurred in a low intensity electric field, two obvious emission peaks at 450 and 520nm (curve a) were presented in the emission spectrum of the product. According to Pang’s research [20], the product was a mixture of two different fluorescent CNPs with blue and green fluorescent property. The introduction of ultrasonic field just led to a limited enhancement of fluorescence intensity (curve b). However, there were still two similar emission peaks in the fluorescence spectrum. Therefore, the electrolysis under a low intensity electric field could not produce pure fluorescent product no matter whether the presence of the ultrasonic filed. The emission spectra of electrolytic product which was obtained under a low intensity electric field in pure water were also measured (Fig. S6 in the supporting information), and two obvious emission peaks at 450 and 520nm were also presented.

However, a single high intensity electric field was also not enough to produce pure fluorescent product efficiently. The effect of ultrasonic field under the high intensity electric field has been also investigated. The electrolysis under a high intensity electric field occurred in pure water. As shown in Fig. 6B, the electrolysis under a high intensity electric field could produce a few amount of fluorescent product without the aid of ultrasonic field (curve a). However, the introduction of ultrasonic field could obviously enhance the electrolytic efficiency. The emission spectrum of the electrolytic product showed only one peak at 450nm (curve b). Benefiting from the combination of high intensity electric field and ultrasonic field, pure blue fluorescent CNPs without green fluorescent CNPs were efficiently generated. The difference of the electrolytic product under the high and low intensity electric field was observed. Under the high intensity electric field, the products were pure blue fluorescent CNPs. Under the low intensity electric field, the products were a mixture of blue fluorescent CNPs and green fluorescent CNPs. The high intensity electric field played a crucial role in improving the purity of the products.

The fluorescent intensity of the electrolytic products was improved under the ultrasonic field, especially under the high intensity electric field at the same time. The effect of ultrasonic field on the fluorescent property of the electrolytic products has also been further investigated in Fig. 7 . When the CNPs’ solution was maintained under a high intensity electric field without the ultrasonic field, the fluorescence intensity was obviously decreased (curve a–b). When the anode of curve b was placed under the ultrasonic field without the electric field, the fluorescence intensity of the solution was increased (curve b–c). This phenomenon could be attributed to the electrophoresis effect. According to the literatures [19,33,34], when the graphite was electrooxidized, abundant carboxyl groups were generated on the surface of graphite. The oxidized graphite layers were easily exfoliated from the graphite surface. The exfoliated graphite layers have hydrophilic carboxyl groups and hydrophobe graphite surface. For the π-π interaction and hydrophilic interaction, the exfoliated graphite layers tended to wrinkle in the form of CNPs. The CNPs were negative charged for its abundant carboxyl groups. Under the high intensity electric field, the electronegative CNPs were moved to the anode and adsorbed on the surface of anode. However, the presence of the ultrasonic field could prevent the electrophoresis effect and released the CNPs into the solution. Curve d was the emission spectrum of the product by use of the naked graphite anode under the ultrasonic field. The fluorescent CNPs would not be achieved under a solo ultrasonic field.

Based on the synergy effect of the high intensity electric field and the ultrasonic field, pure blue fluorescent CNPs were prepared in pure water without any complex purification. The high intensity electric field improved the purity of the products, and the ultrasonic field enhanced the electrolytic efficiency. Therefore, this preparation method was simple, efficient and environmentally friendly. The CNPs showed the high quantum yield and excellent photostability. And the CNPs’ solution was thermodynamically stable. Hence, the CNPs have the potential for application in bioimaging, immuno-labeling and fluorescence detection.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to thank the National Natural Science Foundation of China for financially supporting this research under grant numbers 21127008 and 21105133, and Major National Scientific Instrument and Equipment Development Project (2011YQ03012409), respectively.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2013.08.044.
